function.name: cancel_pending_order
function.description: Cancel a pending order. If the order is already processed or delivered, it cannot be cancelled. The agent needs to explain the cancellation detail and ask for explicit user confirmation (yes/no) to proceed. If the user confirms, the order status will be changed to 'cancelled' and the payment will be refunded. The refund will be added to the user's gift card balance immediately if the payment was made using a gift card, otherwise the refund would take 5-7 business days to process. The function returns the order details after the cancellation.

order_id.description: The order id, such as '#W0000000'. Be careful there is a '#' symbol at the beginning of the order id.

reason.description: The reason for cancellation, which should be either 'no longer needed' or 'ordered by mistake'.

response.description: The order record after a successful cancellation, including updated status, cancel reason, and appended refund entries in payment_history.

order_id.description: The unique ID of the order, such as '#W2611340'.

user_id.description: The ID of the user who placed the order.

items.description: List of items included in the order.

name.description: Display name of the item, such as 'Water Bottle'.

options.description: Item options such as color, size, material, etc.

fulfillments.description: Fulfillment information including shipping tracking and which items are covered.

tracking_id.description: List of tracking IDs associated with this fulfillment.

item_ids.description: List of item IDs included in this fulfillment.

status.description: The current status of the order. After successful cancellation this will be 'cancelled'.

cancel_reason.description: The reason used for cancellation, such as 'no longer needed' or 'ordered by mistake'.

payment_history.description: History of payment-related transactions for the order. Includes original 'payment' entries and appended 'refund' entries after cancellation.

amount.description: Amount of the transaction. Refund amounts are positive here but semantically represent funds returned.

payment_method_id.description: Identifier of the payment method used for this transaction (e.g., 'gift_card_1725971').

function.name: exchange_delivered_order_items
function.description: Exchange items in a delivered order to new items of the same product type. For a delivered order, return or exchange can be only done once by the agent. The agent needs to explain the exchange detail and ask for explicit user confirmation (yes/no) to proceed.

order_id.description: The order id, such as '#W0000000'. Be careful there is a '#' symbol at the beginning of the order id.

item_ids.description: The item ids to be exchanged, each such as '1008292230'. There could be duplicate items in the list.

new_item_ids.description: The item ids to be exchanged for, each such as '1008292230'. There could be duplicate items in the list. Each new item id should match the item id in the same position and be of the same product.

payment_method_id.description: The payment method id to pay or receive refund for the item price difference, such as 'gift_card_0000000' or 'credit_card_0000000'. These can be looked up from the user or order details.

response.description: The updated order record after requesting an exchange. Includes new status, exchange item mappings, and the price difference.

order_id.description: The unique ID of the order, such as '#W2611340'.

user_id.description: The ID of the user who placed the order.

items.description: List of items included in the order.

name.description: Display name of the item, such as 'Water Bottle'.

options.description: Item options such as color, size, material, etc.

fulfillments.description: Fulfillment information including shipping tracking and which items are covered.

tracking_id.description: List of tracking IDs associated with this fulfillment.

item_ids.description: List of item IDs included in this fulfillment.

status.description: The current status of the order. After a successful exchange request this will be 'exchange requested'.

payment_history.description: History of payment-related transactions for the order.

payment_method_id.description: Identifier of the payment method used for this transaction.

exchange_items.description: Sorted list of original item_ids that the user requested to exchange.

exchange_new_items.description: Sorted list of new item_ids that will replace the original items.

exchange_payment_method_id.description: The payment method ID that will be charged or refunded for the price difference of the exchange.

exchange_price_difference.description: Total price difference between new items and original items for the exchange, rounded to two decimals. Can be positive (user pays more), negative (user is refunded), or zero.

function.name: get_order_details
function.description: Get the status and details of an order.

order_id.description: The order id, such as '#W0000000'. Be careful there is a '#' symbol at the beginning of the order id.

response.description: The full order record if found. Includes address, items, fulfillments, status, and payment history.

order_id.description: The unique ID of the order, such as '#W2611340'.

user_id.description: The ID of the user who placed the order.

items.description: List of items included in the order.

name.description: Display name of the item, such as 'Water Bottle'.

options.description: Item options such as size, color, material, etc.

fulfillments.description: Fulfillment information including tracking and which items were shipped.

tracking_id.description: List of tracking IDs associated with this fulfillment.

item_ids.description: List of item IDs included in this fulfillment.

status.description: The current status of the order, such as 'pending', 'processed', 'delivered', or 'cancelled'.

payment_history.description: History of payment-related transactions for the order.

payment_method_id.description: Identifier of the payment method used for this transaction.

function.name: get_product_details
function.description: Get the inventory details (variants, availability, and pricing) of a product.

product_id.description: The product id, such as '9523456873'. Be careful: product id is different from item id.

response.description: The full product record, including all variant item_ids, options, availability, and pricing. Returns 'Error: product not found' if the product_id does not exist.

product_id.description: The product identifier, matching the input product_id.

variants.description: A mapping of variant item_id → variant details. Each variant represents a specific purchasable SKU.

item_id.description: Unique ID of this purchasable item/SKU.

options.description: Option attributes such as color, size, material, style, etc.

available.description: Whether this specific variant is currently in stock.

price.description: The price of this specific product variant.

function.name: list_all_product_types
function.description: List the name and product id of all product types. Each product type has a variety of different items with unique item ids and options. There are only 50 product types in the store.

response.description: A mapping from product name to product_id for all product types in the catalog. Keys are human-readable product names (e.g. 'T-Shirt'), and values are product IDs (e.g. '9523456873').

additionalProperties.description: The product_id corresponding to the product name key.

function.name: modify_pending_order_address
function.description: Modify the shipping address of a pending order. The agent must explain the modification details and ask for explicit user confirmation (yes/no) before proceeding. Only orders with status 'pending' may be modified.

address1.description: The first line of the new address, such as '123 Main St'.

address2.description: The second line of the address, such as 'Apt 1' or ''.

response.description: The updated order object after the address has been modified. If the order does not exist or is not pending, the function instead returns a string error message such as 'Error: order not found' or 'Error: non-pending order cannot be modified'.

function.name: modify_pending_order_items
function.description: Modify items in a pending order to new items of the same product type. For a pending order, this function can only be called once. The agent needs to explain the modification details and ask for explicit user confirmation (yes/no) to proceed.

order_id.description: The order id, such as '#W5918442'. Be careful there is a '#' symbol at the beginning of the order id.

item_ids.description: The item ids to be modified, each such as '1725100896'. There can be duplicate item ids in the list.

new_item_ids.description: The new item ids to replace the original ones, each such as '1586641416'. There can be duplicates. Each new item id must correspond to the same product as the old item in the same position.

payment_method_id.description: The payment method id to pay or receive refund for the item price difference, such as 'gift_card_0000000' or 'credit_card_0000000'. These can be looked up from the user or order details.

response.description: The updated order object after modifying items. The items list, payment history, and status may change. If the order does not exist, is not pending, items are invalid, or payment fails, the function instead returns an error string such as 'Error: order not found', 'Error: non-pending order cannot be modified', or 'Error: insufficient gift card balance to pay for the new item'.

order_id.description: The unique ID of the order, such as '#W5918442'.

user_id.description: The ID of the user who placed the order.

items.description: List of items included in the order after modification. Some item_ids, prices, and options may have changed.

name.description: Display name of the item, such as 'Action Camera'.

product_id.description: Product identifier associated with the item.

item_id.description: Line-item identifier within the order (variant id).

price.description: Price of this item after modification.

options.description: Item options such as color, size, material, etc.

fulfillments.description: Fulfillment information, including tracking and which items are covered. For pending orders this is usually an empty list.

tracking_id.description: List of tracking IDs associated with this fulfillment.

item_ids.description: List of item IDs included in this fulfillment.

status.description: The current status of the order. After a successful modification this will be 'pending (item modified)'.

payment_history.description: History of payment-related transactions for the order, including the original payment and any additional payment or refund created by this modification.

payment_method_id.description: Identifier of the payment method used.

examples: Error: non-pending order cannot be modified
Error: insufficient gift card balance to pay for the new item

function.name: modify_pending_order_payment
function.description: Modify the payment method of a pending order. The agent needs to explain the modification detail and ask for explicit user confirmation (yes/no) to proceed. Only orders with status 'pending' and exactly one original "payment" transaction can be modified.

order_id.description: The order id, such as '#W5918442'. Be careful there is a '#' symbol at the beginning of the order id.

payment_method_id.description: The new payment method id for the order, such as 'gift_card_0000000' or 'credit_card_0000000'. These can be looked up from the user details.

response.description: The updated order object after changing the payment method. Includes full order details: address, items, fulfillments, status, and payment history (including the original payment and the new payment + refund added by this operation). If the order or payment method is invalid or cannot be modified, the function instead returns an error string such as 'Error: order not found', 'Error: non-pending order cannot be modified', 'Error: payment method not found', or 'Error: insufficient gift card balance to pay for the order'.

order_id.description: The unique ID of the order, such as '#W5918442'.

user_id.description: The ID of the user who placed the order.

items.description: List of items included in the order.

name.description: Display name of the item, such as 'Perfume'.

options.description: Item options such as size, color, material, etc.

fulfillments.description: Fulfillment information including tracking and which items were shipped. For pending orders this is usually empty.

tracking_id.description: List of tracking IDs for this fulfillment.

item_ids.description: List of item IDs in this fulfillment.

status.description: The current status of the order, e.g. 'pending'.

payment_history.description: History of payment-related transactions for the order. After modification this will include the original payment, a new payment on the new method, and a refund on the old method.

payment_method_id.description: Identifier of the payment method used.

examples: Error: non-pending order cannot be modified
Error: insufficient gift card balance to pay for the order

function.name: modify_user_address
function.description: Modify the default address of a user. The agent must explain what will be changed and ask for explicit user confirmation (yes/no) before calling this function. On success, it returns the full updated user profile; on failure, it returns an error string such as 'Error: user not found'.

user_id.description: The user id, such as 'noah_brown_6181' or 'ivan_santos_6635'.

address1.description: The first line of the address, such as '123 Main St'.

address2.description: The second line of the address, such as 'Apt 1' or ''.

response.description: The updated user profile after modifying the default address. If the user id does not exist, the function instead returns the string 'Error: user not found'.

address.description: User's default shipping address after the update.

payment_methods.description: Stored payment methods keyed by payment method id.

brand.description: Card brand for credit cards, such as 'visa' or 'mastercard'.

last_four.description: Last four digits of the card number.

balance.description: Remaining balance for gift cards, if applicable.

orders.description: List of order ids associated with the user.

function.name: return_delivered_order_items
function.description: Return some items of a delivered order. The order status will be changed to 'return requested'. The agent needs to explain the return detail and ask for explicit user confirmation (yes/no) to proceed. The user will receive follow-up email for how and where to return the item. Only orders with status 'delivered' can be returned.

order_id.description: The order id, such as '#W2611340'. Be careful there is a '#' symbol at the beginning of the order id.

item_ids.description: The item ids to be returned, each such as '6469567736'. There could be duplicate items in the list if multiple quantities of the same item are being returned.

payment_method_id.description: The payment method id that should receive the refund, such as 'gift_card_0000000' or 'credit_card_0000000'. It must be either the original payment method used for the order or a gift card stored in the user's profile.

response.description: The updated order object after marking items for return. On success, the order status will be 'return requested' and the fields 'return_items' and 'return_payment_method_id' will be added. If the order or payment method is invalid, or the order is not 'delivered', the function instead returns an error string such as 'Error: order not found', 'Error: non-delivered order cannot be returned', 'Error: payment method not found', 'Error: payment method should be either the original payment method or a gift card', or 'Error: some item not found'.

order_id.description: The unique ID of the order, such as '#W2611340'.

user_id.description: The ID of the user who placed the order.

items.description: List of items included in the order.

name.description: Display name of the item, such as 'Water Bottle'.

options.description: Item options such as size, color, material, etc.

fulfillments.description: Fulfillment information including tracking and which items were shipped.

tracking_id.description: List of tracking IDs for this fulfillment.

item_ids.description: List of item IDs in this fulfillment.

status.description: The current status of the order, e.g. 'delivered', 'return requested', etc.

payment_history.description: History of payment-related transactions for the order. For this function, it is not modified, but is returned as-is.

payment_method_id.description: Identifier of the payment method used.

return_items.description: Sorted list of item ids that the user has requested to return.

return_payment_method_id.description: Payment method id that should receive the refund for the returned items.

examples: Error: non-delivered order cannot be returned
Error: payment method should be either the original payment method or a gift card

reason_matrix: self — ignored

After cancelling a pending order, it’s no longer delivered, so an exchange-on-delivery flow is not a natural immediate next step; also this is a high-friction action unrelated to a completed cancellation.

Typically you would check order status/details before cancelling, not after. After a successful cancellation, fetching details is redundant unless there’s a dispute, which is not a routine immediate follow-up.
Product inventory/variant lookup isn’t a typical next step after cancelling an order; it’s only relevant if the customer now wants to shop/replace, which is a separate workflow.
Listing all product types is a browsing/catalog action and not a routine operational follow-up to cancelling a specific pending order.
Once the order is cancelled, it is not pending and its shipping address can’t be modified; this follow-up would just error and is not a normal next step.
Once the order is cancelled, it is not pending and items cannot be modified; also the “modify items” tool is a consequential action that doesn’t fit after cancellation.
Once cancelled, the order is not pending and payment cannot be modified; this follow-up would be invalid and not part of a normal post-cancellation workflow.
Changing the user’s default address is unrelated to cancelling a specific order; not a typical immediate follow-up unless the user separately asks to update profile address.
A cancelled order is not delivered, so return-items flow doesn’t apply; not a plausible immediate post-cancellation step.

After an exchange request on a delivered order, cancelling a pending order is a different workflow and usually a different order/status; also cancellation is a high-friction action with no direct trigger from an exchange outcome. Would require a different pending order_id and cancel reason.
self — ignored
After placing an exchange request, the user often asks to confirm the updated status, exchanged items, or price difference. Agent can fetch the latest record using order_id.

To pick valid replacement variants or confirm availability/pricing for the exchange, the agent may immediately look up inventory for the relevant product. Requires product_id (from the exchanged order’s items).
Listing all product types is not a typical immediate follow-up after an exchange request; the agent already knows the product(s) involved from the order. Only useful in broad browsing scenarios.

An exchange is for a delivered order; modifying a pending order’s address is unrelated and would require a different pending order_id. No common immediate follow-up from a completed delivered-order exchange.

Modifying items in a pending order is a different workflow/status than exchanging delivered items, and would require a different pending order_id. Not a natural next step right after requesting an exchange.
Modifying payment on a pending order is unrelated to exchanging delivered items and depends on a different eligibility rule (pending + single original payment). Not a typical immediate follow-up.

Changing the user’s default address is not directly related to completing an exchange request for a delivered order; the exchange uses the existing order context. Would require user_id and new address details, usually a separate request.

If the user changes their mind after discussing exchange, a common alternative is to return the delivered items instead. Agent would use order_id, item_ids to return, and refund payment_method_id (original method or an existing gift card).

After viewing order details, a common next step is cancelling if the status is 'pending'. Agent would request/confirm: order_id and cancellation reason ('no longer needed' or 'ordered by mistake') plus explicit user confirmation.

After seeing the order is 'delivered' and which item_ids are in it, the user may ask to exchange items (size/color). Agent would request/confirm: order_id, item_ids to exchange, new_item_ids, payment_method_id, and explicit user confirmation.
self — ignored

If the user wants to change variants (or needs availability/pricing before an exchange/modify), the agent may look up the product inventory for an item’s product_id from the order. Inputs: product_id (read from the order’s items).

Listing all product types is not a typical immediate follow-up to checking a specific order; it’s a broad catalog-browsing step and usually unnecessary once the order already provides product_id/name.

If the customer says the shipping address is wrong and the order is still 'pending', the agent would modify the shipping address after checking current status/details. Inputs: order_id and full new address fields (address1, address2, city, state, country, zip) plus explicit user confirmation.

If the customer wants a different variant/SKU before shipment, the agent may modify pending order items after confirming the order is 'pending' and collecting item_ids/new_item_ids. Inputs: order_id, item_ids, new_item_ids, payment_method_id, plus explicit user confirmation.

If the user wants to change how they pay (e.g., use a different card/gift card) and the order is 'pending', the agent would proceed after verifying payment history/status. Inputs: order_id, new payment_method_id, plus explicit user confirmation.

Modifying the user’s default address is not a typical immediate next step from inspecting an order; most address-related order issues are handled via ModifyPendingOrderAddress, and changing the default profile address is a separate request.

After confirming the order is 'delivered' and identifying the relevant item_ids, a common next step is initiating a return. Inputs: order_id, item_ids to return, refund payment_method_id (original or a stored gift card), plus explicit user confirmation.

After viewing product variants, cancelling an order is a separate, high-impact action and wouldn’t be a routine immediate next step unless the user explicitly switches to an order issue; would require order_id and reason.

Common flow: user is exchanging an item and needs to see which replacement variants are in stock and their item_ids/prices; right after product details, agent may initiate the exchange using order_id, item_ids, new_item_ids, payment_method_id (and user confirmation handled outside tool call).
After checking product details (e.g., to identify item_ids/options), the agent often needs to pull the customer’s order to see what they bought/status and line item_ids; requires order_id.
self — ignored
If the user doesn’t know the product_id and started with “what options do you have?”, the agent may list product types first to find the right product_id; no user inputs required.
Changing a pending order’s shipping address is unrelated to looking up product inventory; would only follow if the user pivots to address correction; requires order_id and full new address fields.

Common in modify-item workflows: after seeing which variant item_ids are available for a product, the agent can modify a pending order’s items to selected new_item_ids; requires order_id, item_ids, new_item_ids, payment_method_id (plus user confirmation).

Not directly tied to product inventory; could happen if the user pivots to updating their default profile address, but that’s not a typical immediate follow-up to product details; requires user_id and full address fields + confirmation.

Returns are an order-level post-delivery action; product inventory details usually aren’t needed to return items (more relevant for exchanges). Would require order_id, item_ids, payment_method_id and explicit intent.

If product details reveal unavailable/incorrect variant pricing and user wants to switch payment method for a pending order, agent might proceed to payment change after confirming; requires order_id and new payment_method_id (plus confirmation).
Listing product types is a catalog-browsing step and doesn’t naturally lead straight to canceling an order; cancellation would require an order-specific intent plus order_id and cancel reason.

Listing product types doesn’t directly enable exchanging items in an existing delivered order; exchange typically follows order troubleshooting and requires order_id, item_ids, new_item_ids, and payment_method_id.
After listing product types, it’s not a routine next step to fetch an order; get_order_details is order-centric and would require the user to provide an order_id for a separate intent.

A common follow-up after listing all product types is the user picking one and asking what variants/options exist; the agent would then call get_product_details using the chosen product_id (from the list).
self — ignored
Knowing product types doesn’t naturally lead to changing a pending order’s shipping address; that workflow is order-specific and needs order_id plus the full new address fields.

Listing product types can help select a product category before changing variants, but modifying pending order items is a consequential, order-specific action and not an immediate routine follow-up; it requires order_id, item_ids, new_item_ids, and payment_method_id.
Changing payment method is unrelated to catalog browsing; modify_pending_order_payment is order-specific, high-friction, and requires order_id and payment_method_id.
Updating the user’s default address is not a typical next step after listing product types; it’s profile management and requires user_id plus the full new address fields.
Returns are unrelated to listing product types; return_delivered_order_items is an order-specific, consequential action requiring order_id, item_ids, and payment_method_id.

After changing the shipping address, a common next request is “actually just cancel it.” Agent would use the same order_id and ask the customer for cancellation reason ('no longer needed'/'ordered by mistake') plus explicit confirmation.

Address modification is for pending orders; exchange applies only to delivered orders and is a one-time, high-friction action. Not a typical immediate follow-up unless the user changes intent to an exchange scenario.
After modifying the address, the agent may immediately verify the update/status with the customer (“can you confirm what it shows now?”). Needs order_id.

Product catalog lookup isn’t a typical next step after an address change unless the user is also modifying items; item changes would normally use the order’s existing items/product_ids without immediately browsing product details. Needs product_id (often not user-provided here).
Listing all product types is unrelated to confirming an address change and would be an unusual immediate follow-up. No direct customer intent link.
self — ignored

After changing the order’s shipping address, the customer may also want to change which items/variants are in the pending order. Needs order_id, item_ids to change, new_item_ids, and payment_method_id for any price difference (plus confirmation).
A frequent adjacent request is changing payment method on the same pending order after updating the address. Needs order_id and the new payment_method_id (plus confirmation).

Customer may want their default profile address updated too so future orders ship correctly (separate from this order). Needs user_id and the full new address fields (address1/address2/city/state/country/zip) plus confirmation.

Returns apply only to delivered orders and are not a typical immediate follow-up to modifying a pending order’s shipping address. High-friction and status-mismatched; would require order_id, item_ids, payment_method_id.

After modifying pending items, the customer may decide they no longer want the order and ask to cancel it; agent would use the same order_id plus a cancellation reason ('no longer needed' or 'ordered by mistake').
Exchange applies only to delivered orders, so it’s not a plausible immediate follow-up to modifying items on a pending order unless the status context changes; that would be a different workflow.
After modification, it’s common to verify the final order contents/status/payment history for the customer; needs order_id.

If the customer asks what other variants/options are available for the product they just swapped (to confirm sizes/colors/stock), the agent would fetch product variants using product_id (from the order’s items) and/or confirmed by the user.
Listing the entire catalog is not a typical immediate next step after modifying items; it’s broad discovery and usually unrelated to completing/confirming the just-made order change.
Customer may also want to change the shipping address on the same pending order right after item changes; needs order_id and the new address fields (address1, address2, city, state, country, zip).
self — ignored
If the customer’s next request is to change how they pay for the (still) pending order after item modification, agent can update payment; needs order_id and new payment_method_id.

Changing the user’s default profile address is not a typical immediate follow-up to an order item modification (order address is separate); only fits if user explicitly asks to update default address; otherwise mismatched intent.
Returns apply to delivered orders; immediately after modifying a pending order’s items, a return request is not applicable without a separate delivered-order context.

After changing payment, the customer may decide they actually want to cancel the same pending order; agent can proceed to cancellation after explaining/refund details and getting confirmation. Inputs needed: order_id, reason.

Payment modification is for pending orders; exchanging items applies only to delivered orders, so it’s not a typical immediate follow-up unless the order status context was wrong. Inputs needed would be order_id, item_ids, new_item_ids, payment_method_id.

If the payment change fails (non-pending order, multiple payments, bad payment method, insufficient gift card balance) or the customer asks to verify the update, the agent would fetch the current order state. Inputs needed: order_id.
Product inventory lookup isn’t a normal next step after changing payment; it relates to item option changes, not payment changes. Would require product_id, which may not be relevant here.
Listing all product types is unrelated to a payment-method change workflow and would be an unusual immediate follow-up. No user inputs, but mismatched intent.

Customers often bundle changes: after updating payment, they may also want to change shipping address on the same pending order. Inputs needed: order_id, address1, address2, city, state, country, zip.

After switching payment, the user may also request to change item variants in the pending order (size/color etc.); agent would gather full item change list then call once. Inputs needed: order_id, item_ids, new_item_ids, payment_method_id.
self — ignored

After changing the order’s payment method, the customer might also ask to update their default profile address for future orders. Inputs needed: user_id, address1, address2, city, state, country, zip.
Returns apply only to delivered orders, so it’s not a plausible immediate next action after modifying payment on a pending order. Inputs needed would be order_id, item_ids, payment_method_id.

After updating the default address, the customer may also want to cancel a specific pending order that was going to the old address. Inputs needed: order_id and cancellation reason ('no longer needed' or 'ordered by mistake'), plus the user's explicit confirmation.

If the user updated their default address because a delivered order went to the wrong place or needs swapping, they may immediately request an exchange. Inputs needed: order_id, item_ids, new_item_ids, payment_method_id (from profile), plus explicit confirmation.

Getting order details isn’t a typical immediate next step after successfully changing the default profile address unless the user also has an order issue; otherwise it’s a separate intent. Would require order_id.
Product-catalog lookup is not a natural follow-up to changing the user’s default address; it’s a shopping/browsing task rather than profile maintenance. Would require product_id.
Listing all product types is unrelated to updating a default shipping address and would be an unusual next action in support flow. No user-provided inputs, but mismatched intent.

Common follow-up: user updated default address and now wants to update the shipping address on an existing pending order too. Inputs needed: order_id and the new address fields (address1, address2, city, state, country, zip), plus explicit confirmation.

After changing default address, the user may also want to modify items in a pending order before it ships (often part of the same “fix my pending order” request). Inputs needed: order_id, item_ids, new_item_ids, payment_method_id, plus explicit confirmation.

After changing default address, the user may also want to change the payment method on a pending order (same contact about updating order details). Inputs needed: order_id, new payment_method_id, plus explicit confirmation.
self — ignored

After updating default address, the user might immediately request a return for a delivered order (e.g., wrong delivery address or needs refund). Inputs needed: order_id, item_ids to return, payment_method_id, plus explicit confirmation.
After returning delivered items, cancelling is not applicable because cancellation is only for orders in 'pending' status; the order is now 'return requested'.

If the user realizes they wanted a replacement instead of a return (or to exchange different variants), the agent may proceed to request an exchange. Inputs: order_id, item_ids to exchange, new_item_ids, payment_method_id.
A common immediate follow-up is verifying the updated order status/return details or re-checking if an error occurred. Inputs: order_id.
Product catalog lookup isn’t a routine next step after initiating a return; returns don’t require checking product variants/pricing unless switching to an exchange flow.
Listing all product types is unrelated to completing a return request; it’s more for browsing/selection rather than post-return workflow.
After returning delivered items, modifying a pending order’s address is not applicable (wrong order status: delivered/return requested, not pending).
After returning delivered items, modifying pending order items is not applicable (requires status 'pending' and is a different workflow).
After returning delivered items, modifying pending order payment is not applicable (requires status 'pending' and is unrelated to the return request).

Changing the user’s default address is not a typical immediate next step after a return request; only relevant if the user separately asks to update their profile address. Inputs would be user_id and new address fields.
self — ignored
is_root_reason: A customer can directly ask to cancel an order and provide the human-facing inputs needed: order_id (e.g., “#W…”) and a cancellation reason (“no longer needed” or “ordered by mistake”). Authentication is handled as a routine prerequisite per policy, and no additional domain-specific discovery is required.

This tool requires internal identifiers (item_ids, new_item_ids, payment_method_id) that customers typically don’t know at the start; agents usually must first look up the delivered order details and product variants to select valid exchange targets and payment method. So it’s not a realistic first action.

A customer can start a support interaction by asking “what’s going on with order #W…?” or “where’s my order?”. The only required input is a customer-provided order_id (after authentication, which is assumed handled), so this can be the first meaningful action.

get_product_details requires a product_id, which customers typically don’t know or provide directly. Without a lightweight, guaranteed way to map from a user’s natural-language product name to product_id in this step (usually needing a prior catalog lookup like list_all_product_types), it’s not a reliable first meaningful action.

A customer can reasonably start with “What products do you sell?” and the agent’s first meaningful action can be to list_all_product_types. The function needs no inputs and returns human-readable product names with product_ids.

A customer can start a support request with “Please change the shipping address on order #...,” providing order_id and the full new address (address1/address2/city/state/country/zip). Authentication is a routine prerequisite handled outside this function, and the remaining inputs are user-facing.

Not a good entry-point because it requires internal-ish identifiers the customer often won’t have upfront (item_ids, new_item_ids, payment_method_id) and typically needs prior steps to look up the order contents/available variants and collect a complete modification list before making the one-time call.

Not a good entry-point because it requires internal payment_method_id (looked up from user profile) and a specific pending order_id; typically the agent must first identify the order and retrieve available payment methods before choosing which payment_method_id to apply.

A customer can reasonably contact support to change their default shipping address, providing the full address details in natural language (address1, address2, city, state, country, zip). After authentication (assumed handled), user_id is available, and the only remaining prerequisite is obtaining explicit confirmation.

Calling return_delivered_order_items requires specific internal identifiers (item_ids within the order and a valid payment_method_id that must match the order’s original method or a stored gift card). In a realistic support entry, the agent usually must first look up order details to identify eligible item_ids/payment method and confirm the order is delivered.
